policy_statement: Adapt fishing practices to promote non-waste and minimise by-catches of vulnerable species, and redirect aquaculture activity towards native species with greater climate resilience.
mechanism_description: The policy adapts fishing to reduce waste and by-catch of vulnerable species and redirects aquaculture toward native, more climate-resilient species to strengthen ecosystem and food system resilience under climate change.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate stressors on oceans → ecosystem & fishery disruption risk → management/practice change → ↓ ecosystem degradation + ↑ stock robustness → ↓ food-system vulnerability
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed human activity/system: Fisheries operations (gear, targeting, discard practices, bycatch management) and aquaculture species selection/production practices are altered to reduce waste/bycatch and favor native, climate-resilient species.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced incidental mortality and habitat/food-web impacts improves population dynamics and biodiversity; stocking/raising species better suited to warmer/variable conditions improves survival and productivity under changing ocean conditions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater ecosystem resilience and more reliable seafood production, reducing sensitivity and exposure of coastal food systems and livelihoods to climate-driven shocks and long-term shifts.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced vulnerability of marine ecosystems and seafood supply by shifting fishing/aquaculture practices toward lower bycatch and more climate-resilient (often native) species.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Climate change increases stress on marine ecosystems; policies modify fisheries and aquaculture practices to maintain ecosystem function and stabilize food supply—this is primarily reducing vulnerability, not primarily cutting emissions.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The stated intent and main effect are “ecosystem and food system resilience”; any mitigation (fuel/emissions reductions, altered feed supply chains) is secondary and not the single biggest effect common to all such policies.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy version mainly targets emissions (e.g., decarbonizing fishing fleets) it would belong elsewhere; however, this canonical mechanism is about ecological resilience via bycatch reduction and climate-resilient species selection, which aligns with Adaptation.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: By definition, policies in this mechanism class improve resilience of fisheries/aquaculture systems under climate change through ecosystem-friendly harvesting and resilient species choice, making Adaptation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Potential mitigation or nature-based co-benefits (biodiversity gains, carbon storage in marine habitats) require policy-level evidence and are not used to change the primary category here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations could be framed as Nature-Based Solutions if they explicitly restore/protect blue-carbon habitats (seagrass, mangroves) as the main lever, or as Mitigation if emissions reductions are the primary objective; absent that, the dominant effect is adaptation via ecosystem and food-system resilience.
key_indicators: “Adapt fishing practices”; “promoting non-waste”; “minimising by-catches”; “new climate framework”; “Redirect aquaculture activity”; “native species”; “greater climate resilience”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation and reduced ecosystem degradation; reduced waste/improved resource efficiency; potential improvements to long-term fisheries livelihoods/food security.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via regulatory mandates (e.g., bycatch limits/gear rules, aquaculture permitting tied to species), but the text itself is framed at a high-level direction without specifying enforceable requirements or incentive mechanisms, so it is classified as a planning/strategy instrument.
